column1: HEADLINE: Massive under-absorption of over KSh 204 billion in infrastructure development budgets threatens project execution
column2: while State Corporations simultaneously choke the economy with KSh 161.5 billion in unpaid contractor bills.

column1: The Railway Development Levy outpaced expectations
column2: collecting KSh 22.9 billion against a target of KSh 19.2 billion (a positive variance of KSh 3.7 billion).

column1: National Government outstanding pending bills sit at a staggering KSh 468.5 billion
column2: presenting a severe risk to supplier liquidity and economic stability.

column3: with KSh 161.5 billion (43.8%) explicitly owed to development projects and contractors.

column1: The State Department for Roads missed its KSh 150.2 billion development spending target by an enormous KSh 111.2 billion
column2: absorbing only KSh 39 billion despite capturing 14.1% of the national development share.

column1: The State Department for Housing and Urban Development similarly failed to absorb KSh 60.8 billion of its KSh 116.7 billion development budget.
The State Department for Transport fell short of its development spending targets by KSh 31.7 billion.
The State Department for Shipping and Maritime Affairs absorbed a mere KSh 83 million out of its KSh 2.2 billion development allocation.
The provided review document operates at a macro-fiscal level and does not detail specific Public Accounts Committee (PAC) or Auditor-General recommendation follow-ups.

Despite Kenyans consistently paying levies like the Railway Development Levy—which actually overperformed its targets—the government is failing to deploy these funds into the economy. With over KSh 204 billion sitting unspent across the roads
column4: infrastructure is stalling. Compounding the pain
column5: the government is withholding KSh 161.5 billion in pending bills meant for contractors
column8: and ensuring that promised roads and affordable houses remain undelivered.
